choices: Participants’ beliefs and actions can influence fundamentals — feedback loops exist
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Perception can change reality in markets.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: Pyramiding in the classic sense means:
choices: Adding to winning positions within rules — not averaging down losers blindly
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Winners ride; losers get cut in many professional playbooks.

teachSlides.title: Loops
teachSlides.body: Optimism can fuel investment, which lifts fundamentals, which fuels optimism — until something breaks. The loop can reverse hard.

teachSlides.title: Pivotal points (Livermore idea)
teachSlides.body: Key levels where a trend proves or fails. Traders wait for the market to confirm — they don’t predict every wiggle.

teachSlides.title: Add to winners carefully
teachSlides.body: If you add size, many pros do it when the market proves them right — not to fix a losing trade.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Feedback loops matter. Pivotal points discipline entries and exits. Pyramiding favors winners — not hope.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Reflexivity challenges which pure theory most directly?
choices: That markets always instantly reflect all fundamentals perfectly
masteryQuestions.explanation: Feedback loops can move prices and fundamentals together.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Averaging down on a losing stock without a new thesis often:
choices: Conflicts with “add to winners” discipline — risk can snowball
masteryQuestions.explanation: Many pros cut losers and add to strength.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A pivotal point is best used as:
choices: A place where the market’s behavior confirms or breaks your plan
masteryQuestions.explanation: It’s a decision zone, not a promise.
